images.caption: Figure 1:Forward bias folding for a dead-incoming neuron. When rowkkofW1W_{1}is structurally zero, the neuronkk’s post-activation output is the constantck=φ​(BN​(b1​[k]))c_{k}=\varphi(\mathrm{BN}(b_{1}[k])). Removing neuronkkwithout changing the network’s input–output map requires addingW2​[:,k]​ckW_{2}[:,k]\,c_{k}intob2b_{2}, after which rowkkofW1W_{1}and columnkkofW2W_{2}can be dropped.

Figure 2:ConvNeXt block under the three minimization modes.Mode A(inverted bottleneck reduction) reduces the inner4​C4Cdimension toCA<4​CC_{A}<4Cby modifyingPWConv1andPWConv2.Mode B(pre-bottleneck reduction) reduces the pre-bottleneck width toCB<CC_{B}<Cby modifyingDWConvandPWConv1;LayerNormis replaced withCompensated LayerNorm, which reproduces the full-width LN output from stored sufficient statistics of the removed constant channels (seeSection3.3).Mode C(full block reduction) removes a block which does not consume any input anymore; the resulting constant output is folded backward into the predecessor’s bias (stemLN, downsample conv, or previous block’sPWConv2).
Amber color mark layers modified relative to the unreduced block.

Figure 3:The Squeeze-Release cycle.
Squeeze is a function-preserving structural rewrite that removes dead-incoming and dead-outgoing units.
Release restores trainable capacity at zero size cost.
Iteration finds structural redundancy that single-pass minimization cannot.
Cycle is stopped when termination conditions are achieved - either the network size does not decrease, or the max cycle limit has been reached.
